text.title: Stat Power Pre-Check Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that prevent underpowered CRO experiments from being launched. Each rule is a refusal gate; STRETCH or KILL verdicts are valid outputs, not failures of the methodology.

rule.statement: Before launching ANY CRO experiment, the marketer MUST record five inputs: (a) weekly unique visitors to the test surface, (b) baseline conversion rate over the last 4 weeks, (c) target relative lift expressed as a percentage, (d) significance alpha (default 0.05), (e) desired statistical power (default 0.80). Missing any one input → verdict cannot be issued → experiment cannot launch.
rule.rationale: Power analysis is a function of these five inputs. Any one missing makes the verdict guesswork. Forcing the marketer to fill them all converts vague intent ("let's try this") into a calibrated decision, even when the math itself is delegated to a calculator.

rule.statement: The experiment MUST be tagged with a verdict (GO / STRETCH / KILL) recorded in the experiment ledger before the test goes live. GO = stated lift detectable within the agreed window at stated power. STRETCH = detectable only if window extended OR power lowered to ≥0.70; the extension MUST be approved and logged. KILL = stated lift undetectable in any reasonable window; pivot to a structural change or qualitative method.
rule.rationale: Without a recorded verdict, post-hoc rationalisation reigns: any direction of result will be claimed as the intended one. A pre-registered verdict, even informal, anchors the post-test conversation in the original power expectation.

rule.statement: Each variant MUST be projected to collect at least 200 conversion events over the test window before the experiment is GO. If the projection is below 200/variant the verdict is KILL regardless of what the parametric calculator returns. Use qualitative methods (5-second tests, interviews, session replays) instead.

rationale.source: Practical CRO floors; rule-of-thumb from Evan Miller and CXL Institute

rule.rationale: Below ~200 conversions per variant, even theoretically detectable lifts are dominated by week-to-week variance and seasonality. The parametric calculator's confidence is misleading at low counts. The floor is a heuristic guard against false precision.

rule.statement: The test MUST run to the pre-computed sample size before any significance check is performed, OR use an explicit sequential-testing method (Bayesian / mSPRT / always-valid p-values) that controls error under repeated peeking. "Peeking daily and stopping when p < 0.05" is FORBIDDEN and invalidates the verdict.

rationale.source: Johari, Pekelis, Walsh 2017 on peeking; Optimizely Stats Engine

rule.rationale: Repeated significance checks on fixed-horizon tests inflate the Type-I error far above the nominal alpha. A test designed for alpha=0.05 with daily peeking can have actual false-positive rate of 30%+. The rule is a hard procedural gate, not a preference.

rule.statement: Target lift MUST be expressed as a RELATIVE percentage on the baseline rate (e.g. "10% lift on a 2% baseline = detect 2.2%"), NOT as absolute percentage points. Calculators that take absolute points MUST receive the correctly converted absolute delta; the AGENTS.md / ledger entry MUST show both.
rule.rationale: Confusing relative percentage with absolute percentage points is the single most common error in CRO power inputs. "10% lift" on a 2% baseline is a 0.2pp delta — about 5x the sample size of a 1pp delta. Forcing both forms in the ledger catches this before launch.

example.title: GO verdict — primary landing page, signup flow
example.code:
inputs: weekly_visitors: 8000 baseline_conversion: 3.0% (4-week mean, stable ±15%) target_lift_relative: 15% (3.0% → 3.45%) target_lift_absolute_pp: 0.45pp alpha: 0.05 power: 0.80
calculator: Evan Miller two-proportion required_sample_per_variant: ~5,400 visitors projected_conversions_per_variant: ~162 over 1 week, ~648 over 4 weeks
window: 4 weeks verdict: GO notes: comfortably above the 200-conversions floor; no peeking; ledger entry created
example.note: r1-r5 all satisfied. Both relative and absolute form recorded. Conversions floor cleared at week 2.

example.title: KILL verdict — boutique site, contact-form conversion
example.code:
inputs: weekly_visitors: 600 baseline_conversion: 1.0% target_lift_relative: 10% target_lift_absolute_pp: 0.10pp alpha: 0.05 power: 0.80
calculator: required_sample_per_variant: ~155,000 visitors projected_conversions_per_variant: ~6 over 1 week
window: any reasonable window verdict: KILL notes: 200-conversions floor unreachable within 6 months; switch to 5-second tests + 5 user interviews on the new headline; revisit with a structural redesign, not an A/B
example.note: r3 (conversions floor) hard-fails — the parametric calculator would say "run for 6 months", which is operationally absurd. KILL is the correct verdict.
